title: Why return is better than print for backend logic
learning_goal: Explain why backend functions should return values callers can use.
why_it_matters: API routes, service functions, tests, and background jobs need reusable values. Printing is visible to a person, but it does not give the caller data to work with.

concept_tags.name: Return Values
concept_tags.description: Returning data instead of only printing side effects.

concept_tags.name: Backend Logic
concept_tags.description: Logic that can be tested and reused by API routes.

blocks.title: Learning goal
blocks.body: Explain why return gives backend code a reusable result while print only creates visible output.

blocks.title: Why this matters in real backend work
blocks.body: An API route needs a value it can put into a response, validate in a test, or pass to another service. Printed output disappears into logs or the console.

blocks.title: Core concept
blocks.body: Use return when the caller needs the result. Use print only when you intentionally want a side effect for a human or log stream.

blocks.title: Good example
Bad example
Common beginner mistake
blocks.body: Seeing correct console output can feel like the function worked, but backend code usually needs a value that another function can inspect.

blocks.title: Quick check
blocks.body: Which function result can an API route reuse?

blocks.title: Explain-back
blocks.body: Explain the difference between return and print as if you were reviewing a junior developer's backend function.

blocks.title: Debug challenge
blocks.body: Find why the assertion fails and describe the fix.

blocks.title: Mini task
blocks.body: Rewrite a function that prints a discount message so it returns the message instead.

blocks.title: End checkpoint
blocks.body: Use this checklist before publishing understanding.

items: I can state what return gives to the caller.
I can explain why print is a side effect.
I can fix a function that accidentally returns None.
I can connect this to API route behavior.

questions.prompt: Which design is easiest for an API route to test and reuse?
questions.explanation: Returned values can be inspected by callers, tests, and route handlers.
questions.sample_ideal_answer: Returning values keeps backend logic composable and testable.
questions.remedial_prompt: What can a caller do with a returned value that it cannot do with printed output?

options.label: A
options.text: A function that prints the result immediately.
options.explanation: Printing creates output but does not give the caller a reusable value.

options.label: B
options.text: A function that returns the result to the caller.
options.explanation: Returning keeps the value available to the caller.

options.label: C
options.text: A function that always stores results in a global variable.
options.explanation: Global state is harder to test and reason about.

questions.prompt: Explain why return is usually better than print for backend logic.

rubric.strong: Mentions returned data, caller reuse, and testability.
rubric.stable: Explains return versus print with at least one backend consequence.
rubric.weak: Only says return is better without explaining why.

questions.sample_ideal_answer: Return gives the caller a value it can pass to an API response, check in a test, or reuse in another service. Print only writes visible output and usually returns None.
questions.misconception_notes: Watch for answers that treat console output as proof that backend logic produced reusable data.
questions.remedial_prompt: Where would a FastAPI route use a returned value?

debug_tasks.title: Debug the hidden None
debug_tasks.prompt: Explain why the assertion fails, then describe the corrected function behavior.
debug_tasks.broken_code:
def calculate_total(price, tax):
    print(price + tax)

total = calculate_total(10, 2)
assert total == 12
debug_tasks.hint: Look at what calculate_total gives back to its caller.
debug_tasks.expected_fix_summary: The function should return price + tax instead of only printing it.

mini_tasks.title: Return the message
mini_tasks.prompt: Write a small function that builds a discount message and returns it instead of printing it.
acceptance_criteria: A caller variable receives the string.
The code can be explained in one sentence.
